elements.sent-2411-001: Autocorrelations in MCMC chains increase the variance of the estimators they produce.
elements.sent-2411-002: We propose the occlusion process to mitigate this problem.
elements.sent-2411-003: It is a process that sits upon an existing MCMC sampler, and occasionally replaces its samples with ones that are decorrelated from the chain.
elements.sent-2411-004: We show that this process inherits many desirable properties from the underlying MCMC sampler, such as a Law of Large Numbers, convergence in a normed function space, and geometric ergodicity, to name a few.
elements.sent-2411-005: We show how to simulate the occlusion process at no additional time-complexity to the underlying MCMC chain.
elements.sent-2411-006: This requires a threaded computer, and a variational approximation to the target distribution.
elements.sent-2411-007: We demonstrate empirically the occlusion process' decorrelation and variance reduction capabilities on two target distributions.
elements.sent-2411-008: The first is a bimodal Gaussian mixture model in 1d and 100d.
elements.sent-2411-009: The second is the Ising model on an arbitrary graph, for which we propose a novel variational distribution.
elements.sent-2411-010: One of the principal motivations to use a Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) method is to estimate expectations with respect to a given distribution $P$.
elements.sent-2411-011: This is done by averaging functionals over the chains which the MCMC method produces.
elements.sent-2411-012: It is well known that autocorrelations in the chain result in increased variance in the ensuing estimator.
elements.sent-2411-013: Our proposed algorithm, the occlusion process, combines MCMC with a variational approximation to the target with the aim of achieving consistent estimators with reduced variance.
elements.sent-2411-014: Specifically, the occlusion process takes as input a partition of the state space into disjoint regions and is constructed upon an existing MCMC sampler.
elements.sent-2411-015: It monitors the Markov chain produced by the sampler along with the region it is in and, where possible, it produces a sample from $P$ restricted to the region.
elements.sent-2411-016: Upon this event it will use the sample instead of the state from the Markov chain in the estimator, hence occluding the Markov chain state from view.
elements.sent-2411-017: Compare the variance of a functional averaged over the run of a positive Markov chain in a given region, and the variance of that functional averaged over independent draws from $P$ restricted to that region.
elements.sent-2411-018: It is clear that the latter will be smaller than the former.
elements.sent-2411-019: This is the way in which the occlusion process aims to reduce variance.
elements.sent-2411-020: The process uses a variational distribution, and is designed to exploit parallel computation to boost its performance.
elements.sent-2411-021: Given a variational distribution and a threaded computer, the process is straightforward to implement, and its running time is the same as the MCMC sampler it is constructed on by design.

elements.sent-2411-037: Variance reduction methods constitute a historied subgenre of computational statistics.
elements.sent-2411-038: They take existing algorithms and modify them to produce estimators with the same expectation but reduced variance.
elements.sent-2411-039: The change in computational work between the algorithm and its reduced variance modification will be sufficiently small such that an overall increase in efficiency is achieved.
elements.sent-2411-040: The variance reduction method most similar to the method we devise here is stratified sampling with proportional allocation \cite[Chs 5 - 5A]{cochran:1977 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: The occlusion process is similar to stratified sampling because the aforementioned partition acts as a stratification, and the proportional allocation comes from the fact that the number of samples used in the estimator from $P$ restricted to a particular region will be proportional to the length of time the Markov chain spends in that region.
elements.sent-2411-042: What separates our method from stratified sampling is that the conditions for stratified sampling are far more restrictive.
elements.sent-2411-043: Namely, stratified sampling assumes we can sample from $P$ restricted to every region and it assumes that we know the mass of every region under $P$.
elements.sent-2411-044: It is known that stratified sampling with proportional allocation produces lower variance estimators than plain Monte Carlo (MC).
elements.sent-2411-045: One might expect to extend this result to our less restrictive context, although interestingly, we find a counterexample.
elements.sent-2411-046: Variational inference (VI) \citep{blei:2017 consists in finding a distribution $Q$ which is close to $P$ in some appropriate sense, but that has desirable properties.}
elements.sent-2411-047: These properties might include, for instance, the ease with which we sample from it.
elements.sent-2411-048: One of the limitations of VI is that, depending on the optimisation procedure to search for $Q$ and the family of distributions we restrict the search to, samples from $Q$ will not be samples from $P$.
elements.sent-2411-049: Therefore previous authors, e.g. \citep{parno:2018, brofos:2022 , have sought to incorporate $Q$ into (asymptotically) exact methods such as MCMC.}
elements.sent-2411-050: This is also the case with a particular implementation of our method, which uses $Q$ as the proposal distribution in rejection samplers whose samples are used to occlude those of a Markov chain.
elements.sent-2411-051: Parallel computing allows users to perform more computational operations in a fixed length of time.
elements.sent-2411-052: MCMC is a prima facie serial operation, and hence exploiting parallel threads is an attractive prospect since we are able to do additional computations alongside the Markov chain, which may need to be run for a long time to reach equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-053: These additional computations may take the form of other Markov chains \citep{surjanovic:2023, hoffman:2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-054: In our case we devote the additional computing capacity to the rejection samplers mentioned above.
elements.sent-2411-055: It should be noted that the occlusion process shares some similarities with the Kick-Kac samplers conceived in \citep{douc:2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-056: These samplers partition $\mathsf{X}$ into two measurable regions $\{\mathsf{X _1, \mathsf{X _2\}$, and they attempt to take independent samples from $P$ restricted to $\mathsf{X _1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Absent this ability, they instead use a Markov chain which is invariant to $P$ restricted to $\mathsf{X _1$.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Kac's theorem \citep{kac:1947 dictates that if we average a functional over a $P$-invariant Markov chain, beginning at one of these independent samples and ending when it re-enters $\mathsf{X _1$, we get an unbiased estimator of the expectation of the functional with respect to $P$.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Whilst the setup is very similar to that of the occlusion process, we note the following differences.
elements.sent-2411-060: Firstly the Kick-Kac samplers use two regions, each with a different purpose.
elements.sent-2411-061: The occlusion process uses any number of regions, and they are all treated in the same way.
elements.sent-2411-062: Secondly, to produce samples from $P$ restricted to $\mathsf{X _1$, the Kick-Kac samplers must either wait for an independent sample, or use a Markov chain which is invariant to it.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Therefore the process either has to wait for what is possibly a long time, or introduce autocorrelations.
elements.sent-2411-064: When the occlusion process achieves a sample $Y_t$ from $P$ restricted to a region, it is guaranteed to be independent from all other random variables given $\rho(X_t)$ (see Figure \ref{fig:DAG_diagram ).}
elements.sent-2411-065: The process itself is not fully dependent on these samples, and so can continue regardless of the probability of their production.
elements.sent-2411-066: Thirdly the Kick-Kac samplers rely on regenerations to build their estimators and so they have a random time complexity, whereas the occlusion process does not rely on such conditions and the time complexity is specified before the start of the algorithm.

1} \to .)$ \item Store $X_t$ along with its region $\rho(X_t)$ \end{enumerate} } Rejection samplers In threads $j \in [C_rej]$ concurrently: \For{$j \in [C_rej]$}{ \begin{enumerate} \item Sample $Y \sim Q$ and $U \sim Unif[0, 1]$ independently \item Determine the constant to use in the rejection sampler: \[ C_{\rho(Y)} := \inf_{i \in [R]}\left\{C_i: \frac{d\tilde{P}}{d\tilde{Q}}(Y) < C_i\right\} \] and determine the region $i:=\rho(Y)$ that $Y$ is in. \item If \[ U \leq \frac{1}{C_i}\frac{d\tilde{P}}{d\tilde{Q}}(Y) \] then append the sample to $\{Y_{ij}\}_{j = 1}^{N_{i}}$ (such that $N_i \leftarrow N_i + 1$), otherwise go to step 1. \end{enumerate} } Postprocessing _{t=1}^n$, the regions it visits $\{\rho(X_t)\}_{t=1}^n$, collections of samples $\{\{Y_{ij}\}_{j = 1}^{N_i}\}_{i=1}^R$ with $Y_{ij} \sim P_i$ for all $j \in [N_i]$ and $i \in [R]$} \Output{The occlusion estimator $\hat{\mu}_occ$ defined in (\ref{eqn:mu_hat_occ})} Initialise the indicator sequence $\{S_t\}_{t = 1}^n \leftarrow \{0\}_{t = 1}^n$ and the sequence $\{Y_t\}_{t=1}^n \leftarrow \{NaN\}_{t=1}^n$ \For{$i \in [R]$}{ \begin{enumerate} \item Get the set of times the Markov chain $\{X_t\}_{t=1}^n$ was in region $i$: \[ \mathcal{T}_i := \{t: X_t \in \mathsf{X}_i, t \in [n]\} \] and the amount of time $T_i := |\mathcal{T}_i|$. \item if $N_i \geq T_i$ set $S_t \leftarrow 1$ and $Y_t \leftarrow Y_{it}$ for all $t \in \mathcal{T}_i$. \item else: \begin{enumerate} \item Sample a subset $\mathcal{T}'_i \subseteq \mathcal{T}_i$ uniformly from the subsets of size $N_i$ in $\mathcal{T}_i$. \item Set $S_t \leftarrow 1$ and $Y_t \leftarrow Y_{it}$ for all $t \in \mathcal{T}'_i$. \end{enumerate} \end{enumerate} } Output \[ \hat{\mu}_{occ}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}f_{occ}(X_{t},S_{t},Y_{t}) \] where $f_{occ}(X_{t},S_{t},Y_{t}):=\mathds{1}\{S_{t}=0\}f(X_{t})+\mathds{1}\{S_{t}=1\}f(Y_{t})$

elements.sent-2411-294: The Ising model describes the behaviour of magnetic spins $\sigma_i \in \{-1, 1\}$ at nodes in a graph $(V, E)$.
elements.sent-2411-295: Classically the graph in question is a regular cubic lattice although the model has been generalised to arbitrary graphs \citep{delgado:15, bresler:15, mosseri:15 .}
elements.sent-2411-296: We assume that no external magnetic field is present, and that the graph is finite with $|V| = N$ vertices.
elements.sent-2411-297: A single state $\sigma\in\{-1,1\}^{N}$ describes the spin configuration over all vertices, and its potential energy can be written as $U(\sigma)=-\sum_{i=1 ^{N}\sum_{j\in S_{i J_{ij}\sigma_{i}\sigma_{j}$, where $S_{i}$ is the set of $i$'s neighbours, and $J_{ij}\in\mathbb{R}$ describes the interaction strength between vertices $i$ and $j$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-298: Here $P$ is the distribution over spin configurations and is defined with \begin{equation}\label{eqn:Ising_measure} P(A):=\sum_{\sigma\in A}\frac{\exp(-\beta U(\sigma))}{Z(\beta)}\end{equation} for all subsets $A\subseteq\{-1,1\}^{N}$, where $\beta\in[0,\infty]$ is the inverse temperature and $Z(\beta):=\sum_{\sigma\in\{-1,1\}^{N }\exp(-\beta U(\sigma))$ is the so called `partition function' i.e. the normalising constant.}
elements.sent-2411-299: We assume that $J_{ij >0$ for all $i,j\in[N]$ and hence that the system is ferromagnetic.}
elements.sent-2411-300: For such a model neighbouring spins align since doing so decreases the potential energy of the system.
elements.sent-2411-301: We also assume that $J_{ij = 1$ for all $(i,j)\in E$.}
elements.sent-2411-302: Note that for all $A\subseteq \{-1,1\}^N$ we have that $P(A) = P(-A)$.
elements.sent-2411-303: Therefore any sampler on $P$ should spend an equal amount of time in $A$ and $-A$ in the large sample limit.
elements.sent-2411-304: If the temperature $\beta^{-1}$ is very low, the strength of alignment between neighbouring spins is very high.
elements.sent-2411-305: Therefore in this regime, if the graph is sufficiently well connected, the sampling algorithm should spend most of its time in configurations where a large proportion of the spins are aligned.
elements.sent-2411-306: It should spend about half the time with the overwhelming majority of the spins aligned in the positive direction, and half of the time with the overwhelming majority of the spins aligned in the negative direction.
elements.sent-2411-307: If the temperature is very high, the strength of alignment is greatly reduced.
elements.sent-2411-308: Even if the graph is well connected, the proportion of aligned spins should be closer to a half for the running time of the sampler.
elements.sent-2411-309: A quantity of interest in the Ising model is the average magnetisation.
elements.sent-2411-310: It is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eqn:magnetisation} M := \mathbb{E}_P\left[\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i = 1}^N\sigma_i\right]\end{equation} and describes the average spin across the entire graph measured in a typical configuration.
elements.sent-2411-311: Since the expression in the expectation is an odd function of $\sigma$ and $P$ is even, we are able to exactly calculate that $M = 0$ for all graphs, at all temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-312: This allows us to objectively measure the output of our sampling algorithms, and of the occlusion process.
elements.sent-2411-313: The state space has $2^N$ configurations rendering $Z(\beta)$ very difficult to calculate for large $N$.
elements.sent-2411-314: For this reason practitioners use MCMC methods to sample from the Ising model.
elements.sent-2411-315: One such method is the Metropolis algorithm \citep{metropolis:53 .}
elements.sent-2411-316: Originally conceived to sample from the two-dimensional hard-disk model, the Metropolis algorithm can also be applied to the Ising model.
elements.sent-2411-317: It selects a vertex at random and proposes switching the sign of the spin at that vertex.
elements.sent-2411-318: The switch is accepted with the usual Metropolis acceptance probability.
elements.sent-2411-319: For an exact description of the process see Algorithm \ref{alg:metropolis .}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Metropolis algorithm applied to the Ising model}\label{alg:metropolis} \SetKwInOut{Input}{input}\SetKwInOut{Output}{output} \in \{-1, 1\}^N$, matrix of interaction strengths $J \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}$, inverse temperature $\beta \in [0, \infty]$} \Output{A $P$-invariant Markov chain $\{\sigma^{(t)}\}_{t=1}^n$, where $P$ is as in (\ref{eqn:Ising_measure})} \For{$t \in [n]$}{ \begin{enumerate} \item Sample a vertex $I\sim Uniform[N]$. \item Let $\sigma^{(t)}_prop$ be $\sigma^{(t - 1)}$ with a sign change in the $I$th vertex. \item With probability \begin{equation*} \alpha\left(\sigma^{(t - 1)} \to \sigma^{(t)}_prop\right) := \min\left\{1, \exp\left(-\beta U(\sigma^{(t)}_prop) + \beta U(\sigma^{(t - 1)})\right)\right\} \end{equation*} set $\sigma^{(t)} = \sigma^{(t)}_prop$ otherwise set $\sigma^{(t)} = \sigma^{(t - 1)}$. \end{enumerate} }
elements.sent-2411-320: Say we apply the Metropolis algorithm to the low temperature setting: when it has reached equilibrium it will likely be in a region of the state space with most of the spins aligned.
elements.sent-2411-321: After this point most of the proposal spin flips are rejected because they are in conflict with the spins of their neighbours.
elements.sent-2411-322: Therefore the chain will spend the vast majority of time in this region of the state space, instead of only half the time.
elements.sent-2411-323: Also any estimate we derive from the chain will have a high variance due to the size of the autocorrelations.
elements.sent-2411-324: In the high temperature regime the strength of alignment between neighbouring spin sites will only be weak.
elements.sent-2411-325: Therefore the probability of accepting the individual spin flips proposed by the Metropolis algorithm will be higher and the Markov chain generated will exhibit lower autocorrelations than in the low temperature regime.
elements.sent-2411-326: In order to remedy this problem algorithms have been conceived that switch the signs of spins at a cluster of vertices in the graph in a single step of the Markov chain.
elements.sent-2411-327: This is how the Wolff algorithm works \citep{wolff:89 .}
elements.sent-2411-328: Specifically we select a vertex at random as the starting vertex in a cluster.
elements.sent-2411-329: We then add its neighbours that have a similar sign to the cluster each with probability $1 - \exp(-2\beta J)$.
elements.sent-2411-330: We add their aligned neighbours (so long as they have not been considered before) in a similar fashion, and their neighbours, etc. etc. until there are no unvisited aligned neighbours to consider.
elements.sent-2411-331: Once we have built a cluster in this way, we deterministically flip all of the spins inside the cluster.
elements.sent-2411-332: In order to formally describe the algorithm in \ref{alg:wolff , we define the aligned neighbour function $\mathsf{AN :\mathcal{P (V) \to \mathcal{P (V)$ which takes a subset of vertices of all the same sign spin, and outputs all those neighbours of the subset which are aligned.}}}}
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Wolff algorithm}\label{alg:wolff} \SetKwInOut{Input}{input}\SetKwInOut{Output}{output} \in \{-1, 1\}^N$, matrix of interaction strengths $J \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}$, inverse temperature $\beta \in [0, \infty]$, aligned neighbour function $\mathsf{AN}$} \Output{A $P$-invariant Markov chain $\{\sigma^{(t)}\}_{t=1}^n$, where $P$ is as in (\ref{eqn:Ising_measure})} \For{$t \in [n]$}{ \begin{enumerate} \item Sample a vertex $I\sim Uniform[N]$. \item Initialise the cluster $C = \{I\}$, a set of visited vertices $\mathcal{V} = \{I\}$, a set of aligned neighbours which have not been previously visited $\mathcal{N} = \mathsf{AN}(\{I\})\backslash \mathcal{V}$, and a boolean neighbours that is true if $\mathcal{N}$ is nonempty and $false$ otherwise. \end{enumerate} \While{neighbours}{ \For{$j \in \mathcal{N}$}{ \begin{enumerate} \item With probability $1-\exp(-2\beta J)$ append $j$ to $C$. \item Append $j$ to $\mathcal{V}$. \end{enumerate} } \begin{enumerate} \item Set $\mathcal{N} = \mathsf{AN}(C) \backslash \mathcal{V}$. \item If $\mathcal{N} = \varnothing$ set $neighbours = false$. \end{enumerate} } Set $\sigma^{(t)} = \sigma^{(t - 1)}$ and flip all the signs of the spins at the vertices in the cluster $C$ in $\sigma^{(t)}$. }
elements.sent-2411-333: In an above paragraph we explained why the Metropolis algorithm would work poorly in the low temperature setting.
elements.sent-2411-334: Let's say we use the Wolff algorithm instead.
elements.sent-2411-335: After reaching equilibrium, if the graph is sufficiently connected, most of the spins will be aligned.
elements.sent-2411-336: The value of the $\beta$ parameter will dictate that the cluster inclusion probability $1 - \exp(-2\beta J)$ will be high.
elements.sent-2411-337: The number of aligned visitors will also be high.
elements.sent-2411-338: Therefore the Wolff algorithm will build large clusters whose spins it will flip deterministically, allowing it to traverse large distances in the state space in a single step.
elements.sent-2411-339: In the high temperature regime the strength of alignment between spins is weaker.
elements.sent-2411-340: Hence in equilibrium the size of aligned clusters will generally be smaller, and so the individual moves generated by the Wolff algorithm will involve a smaller amount of spin flips.
elements.sent-2411-341: Therefore in the high temperature regime we expect the Wolff algorithm to exhibit higher autocorrelation than in the low temperature regime.
elements.subsubsection-2411-014: An efficiently simulable variational approximation to the Ising model
elements.sent-2411-342: In order to simulate the occlusion process described in Algorithm \ref{alg:embarrassingly_parallel we need access to a variational approximation to the Ising model which we can easily sample from.}
elements.sent-2411-343: To do this we form a partition of $V=\bigcup_{i=1 ^{k V_{i}$ such that $k$ is small, and define a new graph $(\tilde{V ,\tilde{E )$ where $|\tilde{V |=k$ and $(i',j')\in\tilde{E}$ when there exists an edge in the original graph $(i,j)\in E$ such that $i\in V_{i'}$ and $j\in V_{j'}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-344: The node clusters $V_{i}$ therefore form the nodes in $\tilde{V}$.
elements.sent-2411-345: See Figure \ref{fig:two_Ising_graphs for an example of two graphs: $(V, E)$ and $(\tilde{V , \tilde{E )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-346: We extend the state space to include $\mu\in\{-(1-\epsilon),1-\epsilon\}^{k}$ for a given $\epsilon > 0$ such that each $\mu_{i}$ serves as the mean of the spins in $V_{i}$.
elements.sent-2411-347: We let $\mu$ behave according to the dynamics of an Ising model on $(\tilde{V ,\tilde{E )$ and sample the spins within the clusters $V_{i}$ independently with mean $\mu_{i}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-348: Therefore we dictate that \begin{equation}\tilde{U}(\mu):=-\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j:(i,j)\in\tilde{E}}\tilde{J}_{ij}\mu_{i}\mu_{j}\end{equation} where each $\tilde{J _{ij}\in\mathbb{R}$ possibly depends on the number of edges between subgraphs $i$ and $j$.}
elements.sent-2411-349: Since $k$ is small we can calculate the normalising constant \begin{equation}\tilde{Z}(\tilde{\beta}):=\sum_{\mu\in\{-(1-\epsilon),1-\epsilon\}^{k}}\exp(-\tilde{\beta}\tilde{U}(\mu))\end{equation} where $\tilde{\beta}\in[0,\infty]$ is an inverse temperature hyperparameter.
elements.sent-2411-350: As mentioned before, we sample the spins within each subgraph independently conditional on their means, which are given by $\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-351: We choose a Rademacher distribution such that \begin{equation}q(\sigma\left|\mu\right.):=\prod_{i=1}^{k}\prod_{s=1}^{|V_{i}|}\left(\frac{1+\mu_{i}}{2}\right)^{\frac{1+\sigma_{s}^{(i)}}{2}}\left(\frac{1-\mu_{i}}{2}\right)^{\frac{1-\sigma_{s}^{(i)}}{2}}\end{equation} where $\sigma_{s ^{(i)}\in\{-1,1\}$ is the $s$th spin in the $i$th cluster.}

elements.sent-2411-354: For this we can simply calculate the probabilities of all points in $\{-(1-\epsilon),1-\epsilon\}^{k}$ since we have access to $\tilde{Z (\tilde{\beta )$.}}

elements.sent-2411-384: As expected the Wolff algorithm excels in the low temperature setting whereas the Metropolis algorithm fares very poorly due to the fact that in each instance it never strays from its initial position.
elements.sent-2411-385: In the high temperature setting the variance of the estimates from the Wolff algorithm are lower in general than those from the Metropolis chain, but the difference is not as stark as in the low temperature setting.
elements.sent-2411-386: An explanation as to why the Wolff algorithm does so well in the low temperature setting is offered by the graphs of the lag 1 autocorrelation coefficients (top right).
elements.sent-2411-387: One can clearly see that some of these coefficients are close to -1, explaining why the variance of the resulting estimates is very low.
elements.sent-2411-388: As concerns the performance of the occluded chain versus the Metropolis and Wolff samplers: we can see that in the high temperature setting, in every case the variance of the occluded estimator is much lower than those of the Markov chains.
elements.sent-2411-389: The fact that the lag 1 autocorrelation coefficients of the occluded process are so close to zero, combined with the high proportions of occlusion events as seen in the top level of the top right, explain this fact.
elements.sent-2411-390: In the low temperature setting the story is more mixed.
elements.sent-2411-391: If we compare the performance of the occluded estimator with the Metropolis algorithm, we can see that for $N = 20$ and $50$ the occluded estimator offers a reduction in variance.
elements.sent-2411-392: This is explained by the corresponding reduction in the lag 1 autocorrelation coefficients and the high occlusion proportions.
elements.sent-2411-393: However the low occlusion proportion in the $N = 100$ case means that the occluded chain and the Metropolis chain are basically the same, and so the occluded estimator offers no increase in performance.
elements.sent-2411-394: In this case the Markov chain does not move, and so the lag 1 estimator breaks, as seen in the graph in the top left.
elements.sent-2411-395: Comparing the occluded estimator with the Wolff algorithm in the low temperature regime shows that in the $N = 50$ case using the occluded estimator actually decreases the performance by increasing the bias and the variance.
elements.sent-2411-396: This is explained by the fact that the occlusion events tend to decorrelate subsequent states in the process, whereas the Wolff algorithm produces anticorrelated states.
elements.sent-2411-397: Hence the decorrelations are undesirable in this case.
elements.sent-2411-398: Again, as with the Metropolis algorithm, the number of occlusions in the $N = 100$ case is very few, and there is negligible difference between the performance of the occlusion process and the Wolff algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-399: We would like to note that we have not tuned the parameters of the variational distribution for any of the models presented here.
elements.sent-2411-400: Therefore for a better tuned variational distribution, we would expect the occlusion proportions to be higher, and so at least in the low temperature $N = 100$ case achieve a reduced lower variance of the occluded estimator compared to the Metropolis algorithm.

elements.sent-2411-411: It produces unbiased estimates of expectations under the equilibrium distribution $P$ of $K$.

elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[scale = 0.9]{images/lag1_2_clusters_many_regions.pdf} \includegraphics[scale = 0.9]{images/occlusion_proportion_2_clusters_many_regions.pdf} \includegraphics[scale = 1.3]{images/magnetisation_2_clusters_many_regions.pdf} \caption{Three graphs comparing the performance of the occlusion process with the Metropolis and Wolff algorithms on the Ising model at a variety of temperatures, for a variety of graph sizes, each generated using the stochastic block model with 2 communities. In every case the horizontal axes show the number of vertices $N$ in the graphs. Bottom: the vertical axes denote the algorithm's estimates of the expected magnetisation. Top left: the vertical axes denote the lag 1 autocorrelation coefficient of the magnetisation over the states produced by the algorithms. Top right: the vertical axes show the number of samples from the $P_i$'s in Algorithm \ref{alg:embarrassingly_parallel} divided by the number of states in the Markov chain $n$. We magnify the estimated magnetisation plot for ease of comprehension, the top two plots then help to explain the phenomena in the bottom plot.} \label{fig:Ising_results_2_clusters}
